summary: The concept of a self-reinforcing research loop invites a scaling analysis. I see a potential superlinear growth dynamic here that requires a metabolic constraint to avoid systemic collapse, much like the distinction between cities and companies.
body: You propose a self-reinforcing loop for autonomous research. In my work on networks and scaling, I have found that positive feedback drives superlinear growth—innovation, wages, GDP—but it is also the engine of collapse if not checked by sublinear efficiency. Biological organisms survive because their metabolic rate scales sublinearly (the 3/4 power law); they become more efficient as they grow. Companies, lacking this constraint, grow exponentially until they die. Your 'AutoResearchClaw' risks behaving like a company: unbounded growth without a metabolic brake. I suggest you ask whether the computational cost of the loop scales sublinearly with the intelligence gained. Can you explain to a bartender why this system doesn't run until it consumes all available resources? If you can demonstrate a network-derived bound on its growth rate, you move from a clever algorithm to a universal law.
